planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; bee bee tree is adaptable to a wide range of soil conditions and establishes readily once planted. It grows quickly and produces flowers in July and August when almost no other trees are blooming — an extremely valuable quality for beekeepers and native bee habitat.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; bee bee tree (Korean evodia) is arguably the most valuable pollinator tree for temperate gardens, producing massive, flat-topped panicles of small white flowers in July and August — a critical period when almost no other trees are blooming — that attract extraordinary concentrations of bumblebees, honeybees, native solitary bees, butterflies, and other beneficial insects. A single mature specimen in full bloom is a memorable spectacle of insect activity. Native to China and Korea, it grows as a medium-sized tree 20–30 feet tall with large, pinnate, aromatic leaves that have an interesting citrus-like scent when crushed. In fall, the clusters of small, red to black seed capsules are ornamental and attract birds. The tree was formerly known as Evodia daniellii (or Euodia daniellii) and still appears under the old name in many references. Fast-growing, adaptable to a range of soils, and largely pest- and disease-free in North America where no major insects have adapted to feed on it.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; seven-son flower adapts well to a range of soils and exposures. Its exfoliating bark is particularly ornamental on multi-stemmed specimens where older trunk sections peel in attractive vertical strips of gray, brown, and cream.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to part shade; seven-son flower is one of the most underrated multi-season ornamental shrubs or small trees, native to central China and offering a remarkable sequence of interest from late summer through winter. Fragrant, small white flowers appear in clusters in late August and September — later than virtually any other woody shrub — followed by an unusual second display: the sepals surrounding the spent flowers enlarge and turn bright rose-red in October, extending the colorful season by several weeks and often mistaken for the flowers themselves. The exfoliating bark on mature stems peels in long, curling strips of gray, brown, and cream, creating striking winter interest similar to crape myrtle or birch. The bold, dark-green, distinctly veined leaves are tropical-looking for a hardy shrub. Adaptable to zones 5–9 and to a range of soil conditions, it combines late-season bloom, extended fall color, and ornamental bark in a single low-maintenance plant.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in full sun in well-drained, fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil; Japanese tree lilac is exceptionally tolerant of urban conditions including air pollution, compacted soils, heat, and drought once established. The cultivar 'Ivory Silk' is the most widely planted, offering a more compact, uniform habit than the species.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Japanese tree lilac is the tree-form lilac of North American streetscapes and public gardens, valued for its extraordinary cold hardiness (to zone 3), its tolerance of urban stress, and its massive, creamy-white flower panicles that appear in June — two to three weeks after common lilacs — providing lilac-family bloom when the familiar spring shrubs have long finished. The panicles can reach 12 inches long and are produced in great abundance, creating a white-cloud effect in early summer. The scent is distinctive — not the sweet, classic lilac fragrance but a heavier, privet-like quality that some find pleasant and others less so. The bark is attractive and cherry-like on mature specimens, reddish-brown and lightly exfoliating. Highly resistant to powdery mildew (a common problem on common lilacs) and largely trouble-free in the landscape. The cultivar 'Ivory Silk' is a compact, heavy-blooming selection that is the most commonly available.

planting.note: Plant container-grown or balled-and-burlapped trees in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in spring or fall; Persian ironwood adapts to a wide range of soil types including clay and dry soils once established and is notably more heat- and drought-tolerant than its forest origins might suggest. Its multi-stemmed, vase-shaped habit develops best when lower branches are allowed to sweep to the ground.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Persian ironwood is one of the finest four-season ornamental trees available, native to the Alborz Mountains and Caspian lowlands of northern Iran where it grows as a dominant forest species. In late winter and very early spring (January–March), before any leaves appear, the branches are lit with clusters of tiny, brilliant-red staminate flowers — among the earliest flowers of any woody plant. The leaves that follow are large, wavy-edged, and glossy, producing some of the most spectacular autumn color of any tree in deep shades of scarlet, orange, yellow, and burgundy all simultaneously present on one tree. Through winter, the mottled, exfoliating bark in tones of gray, green, and brown creates a patchwork pattern similar to the London plane tree, giving outstanding year-round bark interest. The wood is among the hardest of all deciduous trees, hence its common name. Increasingly recognized as an outstanding landscape tree that offers something of interest in every season.

planting.note: Root stem cuttings in late spring or summer by allowing cut ends to callus for 1–2 weeks before planting in very well-drained cactus mix; Peruvian apple cactus establishes rapidly from large cuttings. It is damaged at 28°F and killed below 25°F, making it suitable for zones 9–11 outdoors or as a dramatic container specimen moved indoors in winter.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean cactus or sandy soil in full sun; Peruvian apple cactus is a large, multi-branched columnar cactus native to South America and widely planted as an ornamental, living fence, and fruit tree throughout the Caribbean, tropical America, and the Mediterranean. The stout, blue-green to gray-green columns branch freely to form impressive upright clumps reaching 10–25 feet, with prominent ribs and clusters of short spines. In summer, it produces large, showy, white funnel-shaped flowers up to 6 inches across that open at night and are pollinated by bats and hawk moths — a remarkable natural spectacle. The flowers are followed by the edible 'Peruvian apples' — oval red to dark-magenta fruits 2–3 inches long with sweet, translucent white flesh and small black seeds, having a mild, pleasant flavor. Related to and confused with the queen of the night and night-blooming cereus, but distinct. Excellent as a living fence, architectural garden specimen, or screening plant in zone 9–11 landscapes.
